FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (N=7691, mean age 12.0 at Year 2)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time measured from ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey; self-reported hours per day on Facebook, Instagram, Twitter/X with weekday/weekend averaging

FT_IC3.evidence: GSEM sequential modeling with direct association between social media exposure and cannabis experimentation; mediation analysis testing cannabis expectancies as mediator

FT_IC4.evidence: Social media time → cannabis experimentation: B=0.18, 95% CI 0.13–0.24, p<0.001; positive expectancies mediated 19.8%, negative expectancies mediated 13.6%

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective ABCD cohort analysis linking Year 2 social media to Year 4 cannabis outcomes with Year 3 mediator

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Drug and Alcohol Dependence 2025)

FT_EC7_reason: Social media is independent variable predicting cannabis outcomes (cannabis expectancies and cannabis experimentation); does not meet criteria for FT-EC7 which requires smartphone/SM as dependent variable with only demographic predictors
notes: INCLUDE. Prospective mediation study with clearly reported coefficients and indirect effects.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (N=9799, Year 2 data, age 11-12 years)

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization measured as single exposure variable. No separate phone/SM measurement reported. Per E6: 'Cyberbullying alone with no separately-measured phone/SM variable' = NOT_MET

FT_IC3.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET means paper fails at exposure criterion; cannot evaluate FT-IC3

FT_IC4.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET means paper fails prior criterion

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Lancet Regional Health - Americas 2025)

FT_EC7_reason: Paper excluded due to FT-IC2 NOT_MET; FT-EC7 not applicable
notes: EXCLUDE. Cyberbullying is measured as sole exposure; no smartphone or social media variable separately measured. Per coder_prompt E6, cyberbullying alone without separately-measured phone/SM = NOT_MET for FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (N=10,305, Year 3 data, age 12-13 years)

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use measured via 11-item questionnaire covering phone/SM use while in bed (texting, social media, video chatting, browsing); composite mean score calculated across device types

FT_IC3.evidence: Bedtime screen use is predictor of sleep outcomes (sleep disturbance, sleep duration). Paper reports associations between screen use variables and sleep outcomes only. No phone/SM as independent variable linked to non-SM behavioral/clinical outcome; sleep is physical/health outcome, not a separate category meeting the 'at least one non-SM variable' criterion in the context of directionality analysis

FT_IC4.evidence: FT-IC3 NOT_MET means numeric reporting not evaluable for phone/SM association criterion

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD cross-sectional analysis (Year 3); empirical analysis with regression models

FT_EC7_reason: All analyses report bedtime screen use as independent variable predicting sleep outcomes (sleep disturbance, sleep duration). No non-SM/non-sleep outcomes reported. Sleep is physiological outcome. Bedtime screen use could be analyzed as outcome in relation to sociodemographic predictors. However, per FT-IC3 criterion, the main hypothesis structure focuses on screen use → sleep, making this a screen-outcome study rather than smartphone/SM → behavioral/psychological outcome study. Applies E7 logic but FT-IC3 already excludes based on directionality of exposure-outcome framing.
notes: EXCLUDE. FT-IC3 NOT_MET. Paper structure reports bedtime screen use primarily as predictor of sleep outcomes, not smartphone/SM as independent variable with non-sleep/non-physiological outcomes. Focus is sociodemographic patterns and sleep health, not behavioral/psychological/clinical outcomes typically required for inclusion.
